title: June | 2026 | FRED Blog
gist: This is the FRED Blog June 2026 archive/landing page aggregating several posts; beyond duplicating the Treasury-holders post, it carries unique substantive content from two posts not otherwise present here — the 20-year ALFRED vintage-data piece and the US debt-to-GDP ratios explainer. Card captures the non-duplicate data points; entities/quotes drawn only from this page's text.

themes.theme: ALFRED and the value of data vintages

insights.claim: ALFRED, FRED's archival data service launched in 2006, turned 20 and lets researchers see the data that were available on specific past dates.
insights.speaker: St. Louis Fed — FRED Blog
insights.so_what: Backtests and historical analyses should use as-of-date (vintage) data to avoid look-ahead bias from later revisions.
insights.locator: FRED's archival data service has turned 20

insights.claim: The January 6, 2021, release of the Economic Policy Uncertainty Index contained a 300% spike that was an error and corrected the next day.
insights.speaker: St. Louis Fed — FRED Blog
insights.so_what: Single-day data spikes can be errors; vintage data lets you verify before trading on an anomaly.

insights.claim: Between 1991 and 2023 real GDP underwent eight comprehensive updates, each adjusting reference years for inflation.
insights.speaker: St. Louis Fed — FRED Blog
insights.so_what: Headline GDP is periodically rebenchmarked, so cross-vintage comparisons of growth can mislead without accounting for revisions.

insights.claim: Hurricanes Harvey and Irma in 2017 hit 87 counties representing 7.7% of national employment, and the first release of September 2017 employment showed a 33,000-person decline that later revisions flipped to increases of as many as 38,000.
insights.speaker: St. Louis Fed — FRED Blog
insights.so_what: Initial employment prints around disasters can reverse sign on revision — fade knee-jerk reactions to first releases.
insights.locator: An example of data revisions captured in ALFRED

insights.claim: FRED carries multiple US debt-to-GDP series that diverge: two with 2025 values well above 100% and one slightly below 100%, with the gap largely explained by whether federal debt held by agencies and trusts (intragovernmental holdings) is removed.
insights.speaker: St. Louis Fed — FRED Blog
insights.so_what: The '100% debt-to-GDP' headline depends on the series; net-of-intragovernmental debt is meaningfully lower, so cite the right one.
insights.locator: US public debt and intragovernmental transactions

insights.claim: The Gross federal debt as percent of GDP series mixes a fiscal-year numerator (Oct 1–Sept 30, sourced from the Council of Economic Advisers) with a calendar-year GDP denominator, causing it to differ from the calendar-year Treasury-sourced series GFDEBTN and FYGFDPUN.
insights.speaker: St. Louis Fed — FRED Blog
entities: US Treasury Bureau of the Fiscal Service
insights.so_what: Mismatched fiscal vs calendar periods create artificial differences between debt ratios — read the series notes before comparing.

insights.claim: Debt is a stock variable defined at a point in time while GDP is a flow variable measured per period, so combining them into a ratio requires care about what numerator and denominator capture.
insights.speaker: St. Louis Fed — FRED Blog
insights.so_what: Stock/flow mismatches are a recurring trap in macro ratios; understand the timing conventions before drawing conclusions.

insights.claim: As of March 2026 the nine largest foreign holders of US Treasuries — Japan, the UK, Mainland China, France, Canada, Belgium, Ireland, Cayman Islands, and Luxembourg — collectively held about 45% of foreign-held US Treasuries, a share stable since the early 2000s.
insights.speaker: St. Louis Fed — FRED Blog
insights.so_what: Concentration of foreign Treasury holdings is steady in aggregate; rankings (Japan/UK ahead of China) signal where demand shifts.
insights.quote: these nine countries collectively hold about 45 percent of foreign-held U.S. Treasuries, a share that has remained relatively stable since the early 2000s.
insights.locator: Who holds US Treasury securities overseas?
